data.title: RioCan REIT: Remains A Buy At 10x FFO And A 26% Discount To NAV
data.description:
Please note all $ figures are in $CAD, not $USD, unless otherwise noted.

Introduction

RioCan Real Estate Investment Trust (REI.UN:CA) (OTCPK:RIOCF) is one of the largest REITs in Canada and has a strong portfolio of retail and grocery-anchored assets across its portfolio. I’ve been bullish on the name since January 2024 and last highlighted the name in November 2024. Since then, the stock has performed poorly with just a 4% return, including the dividend. However, at the recent Investor Day, I think RioCan highlighted a steady path to continued FFO growth, and the valuation has gotten cheaper in the last year. At under 10x FFO, RioCan remains a ‘buy’.

Recent Results

RioCan put up a decent quarter for its Q3 results that came in largely in line with estimates. On the top line, revenue of $371.2 million was $3.1 million above consensus and represented a 1.9% increase compared to last year. Most of this was driven by rental growth of 5% alongside contributions from residential inventory sales.

When we look at net operating income (or NOI), the company’s NOI clocked in at $185.3 million, a 3.5% increase from $179.1 million last year, as the company experienced better expense management and the benefits of prior leasing activity with NOI margins holding steady around the 50% level. On a same-property NOI basis, RioCan saw a 4.2% increase as the core portfolio saw steady increases as a result of steady rent growth across the company’s retail assets.

When we look at funds from operations (or FFO), which is generally a good measure of profitability (akin to EPS for REITs), FFO came in at $136.7 million, which was roughly flat on a dollar basis, coming in at 46 cents on a per-share basis, where on an FFO per-unit basis, actually increased slightly (thanks to a lower share count). With about 61% of FFO getting paid out as distributions to shareholders, the bulk of the remaining cash flow was reinvested into new properties.

On its RioCan-HBC joint venture, RioCan decided to walk away from another three single-tenanted assets. Regarding the four remaining properties, the company agreed to acquire the JV's 50% interests in Georgian Mall and Oakville Place. Deals with replacement tenants for virtually all the former HBC space have been secured at substantially higher rents, requiring incremental capital of $20-25 million.

RioCan continues to execute on the previously communicated $1.3-1.4 billion repatriation program for 2025-26, virtually all of which is composed of 1) apartment property sales and 2) proceeds received on closing of pre-sold condo units. Beyond this, I think retained cash flow combined with a more moderate non-core disposition program should enable the company to deploy $200 million of capital annually. Potential uses include reinvestment into the core business, strategic acquisitions, and the NCIB.

One reason RioCan will probably have more flexibility going forward is due to its strong balance sheet. While $15.4 billion of debt and debt to EBITDA of 8.8x might sound really high, investors should keep in mind that occupancy is high, most rents are contracted with a stable tenant profile, liquidity is high, and leverage is on its way down.

On liquidity, while the cash balance is minuscule, the company does have $1.1 billion of total liquidity as a result of a flexible revolver with $1.0 billion of capacity. Directionally, leverage trends have been heading lower, as the company’s 8.8x gross leverage figure is down from 9.12x last year and is at the lower end of the target 8.0-9.0x range.

One of the reasons I’m not concerned about higher leverage is because 94% of rents are collected from major Canadian markets (usually in or close to the big cities) where rent growth outpaces the rest of the country. As well, when more than half of your revenues are coming from essential goods and services and grocery/retail anchor tenants, unless we see a deep or severe recession, these tenants will still continue to pay their rent on time. Some of these tenants include names like Dollarama (DOL:CA), Costco (COST), Loblaws (L:CA), and the Big 5 Canadian banks.

Leasing spreads are also performing pretty well. During the quarter, RioCan had record leasing spreads with a blended rate of 20.8%, encompassing 44.1% on new leases and 15.2% on renewals. High leasing spreads essentially show that tenants are willing to pay noticeably more than the rents coming off the books. That means stronger future rental income, better NOI growth, and proof that demand for RioCan’s properties is healthy. In an environment where G&A costs go up every year, the fact that RioCan is able to maintain high lease rates gives it some pricing power in the sense that it can cushion things like inflation or higher interest costs.

Lastly, as you can probably guess with investment-grade tenants in major Canadian markets, occupancy remains strong at 97.8% for the overall portfolio, up 20 bps from last quarter as there still seems to be demand for necessity-based spaces in high-density markets. If you think about it, how often do you see a new grocery store open up in urban markets? Not often. To me, that’s part of RioCan’s ‘moat’, as they can rely on these entrenched, high-traffic tenants to keep occupancy stable and support stronger rents.

Outlook

At its November Investor Day that took place a week or so after Q3 results, RioCan laid out a plan to exit the residential rental business and wind down the remaining condo projects. From now on, every new dollar goes into the core grocery-anchored retail portfolio. Management is guiding to long-term core FFO per unit growth of 5% a year. That breaks down into at least 3.5% from same-property NOI in the retail assets and another 1.5% from reinvesting capital at attractive returns. For the next three years, they are only committing to at least 3.5% core FFO growth because higher interest rates on upcoming debt maturities will create a temporary headwind.

What I like about the guidance is how much of it is already visible. Roughly two-thirds of the 3.5% same-property NOI target comes from contractual rent steps that are locked into existing leases. Most of the balance is mark-to-market on known rollovers where average in-place rents sit 25-30% below current market levels and recent renewals have been running in the mid-teens.

The move to wind down the remaining condo projects will also help the balance sheet. On the conference call, management noted that they expect these sales to bring in some $1.3-1.4 billion in cash, with a large chunk probably going to reduce debt. Some of this is mortgages and might be assumed by buyers, or construction loans are repaid, but I think it’s safe to say that there will be significant capital to recycle into new retail projects and probably move leverage down a half turn (my estimate).

On balance, there’s nothing here that strikes me as aggressive. RioCan never started as a condo or development REIT, and so it makes sense to stick to your bread and butter. There’s steady, incremental upside if leasing spreads stay strong and management notes that “there is serious thirst for space from retailers in this supply-constrained environment. The future opportunity is where we’re going to overachieve, and that will go well beyond the three years.” As a fixed asset with inflation-indexed cash flows, I think the growth target of 3.5% is reasonable if you have an environment where the economy is growing at 2% long-term.

Near term, the story is simple: finish the residential exit, refinance the debt, keep posting mid-teen renewal spreads, and deploy the capital that is coming in. Longer term, the combination of embedded contractual growth, a big mark-to-market opportunity, and a permanent pool of reinvestable cash should let the retail portfolio compound at healthy mid-single-digit rates for many years. It is not a home-run setup, but it is a very high-probability single with plenty of ways to turn into extra bases if execution stays sharp.

Risks and valuation

With guidance for at least $1.55 in FFO in 2025, RioCan trades at a very reasonable 11.9x FFO, but keep in mind that doesn’t include the HBC joint venture or the condo-related income that would contribute an extra 31 cents to FFO. Factoring in these two items, RioCan trades at 9.9x 2025 FFO.

Compared to peers, RioCan also looks cheap. Compared to similar REITs that trade at an average P/FFO multiple of 13.2x, RioCan trades at a discount at 9.9x (source: Bloomberg). While that might be due to the fact that the company has more leverage than many of the names on the list, I think the portfolio is among the highest quality in Canada.

When you think about what retail cap rates are in major Canadian markets, they are nowhere close to 10%, and in fact, Colliers’ market report earlier this year showed that “retail cap rates are now 5-6% in most markets.” Clearly, there’s a disconnect between public and private market values for real estate when you have a REIT like RioCan trading at a 26% discount to its NAV of $24.90 per share.

In terms of the risks to the investment thesis, the big question mark is almost always interest rates. Based on market perception, it’s likely that most of the cuts for 2025 are already done, with a forecasted rate of 2.25% to close out the year (implying a rate hold at the December meeting). However, when looking at 2026, the direction is probably still lower, as predicted by some economists from Morningstar that think we could get to 1.75%. We’ll see.

Regardless, what this all means for RioCan is that its heavy debt load, despite nowhere near bankruptcy risk (its 5-year bonds trade at tight spreads to government 5-year bonds at 62 bps), the refinancing risk could become an issue over the next couple of years (source: Bloomberg). As an example, when rates were low in 2021-2022, RioCan took advantage of that by taking on debt at extremely favorable interest rates. Today, as those come due, the company would likely be faced with higher coupons on its bonds and less preferential rates for mortgages.

Another risk is the sharp slowdown in Canadian immigration. Ottawa has cut permanent resident targets from 500,000 to under 400,000 annually through 2027 and is curbing temporary residents even more aggressively. RioCan’s portfolio is heavily concentrated in the six major cities where immigration and interprovincial migration have been the primary drivers of household formation and retail spending growth for the past decade. While I view the issue as temporary and more of an issue for the retail chains, it does limit growth on an organic basis for RioCan in terms of rent growth.

Bottom Line

Overall, I think RioCan continues to offer good value for investors today and the recent Investor Day highlighted steady NOI and FFO growth that should translate on a per share basis. As the company exits its condo business and starts to incrementally chip away at its leverage, I think the discount to NAV as well as peers should narrow. At under 10x FFO, I think RioCan is a solid name for investors who want a safe, 6.3% yield that’s well covered by a low payout ratio and has some growth upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SoFi: $1.5B Dilution Raises Red Flags As Insiders Keep Selling
data.description:
The SoFi Technologies (SOFI) stock appears to be stuck as it faces a strong $30 resistance level. The stock has been trading in a relatively (given SOFI's historical volatility) narrow range between around $25 and $30, currently being close to the upper edge of the range. I know that SOFI has rallied by around 16% since my previous bearish call, but it is mostly because of the timing. I could have said that I was correct if I wrote the thesis update on November 20, when the share price closed below $25. If we look at how the forward P/E ratio changed since my previous coverage, we can see that the share price grew faster than positive EPS revisions. Moreover, the company recently announced an underwritten public offering of $1.5 billion in common stock, which is a questionable decision given SOFI's financial strength and bottom-line profitability. I think that selling the stock at the strong resistance level is a sound move, especially taking into account mounting uncertainties in the macro environment. Therefore, reiterating Strong Sell looks fair, in my opinion.

Reasons to avoid SOFI

Overvaluation appears to be the biggest reason to avoid SOFI, because it is a $35 billion company with just $640 million TTM net income. Of course, valuation takes into account future growth potential instead of past performance. However, if we compare the current forward P/E ratio trajectory with the one that we had in late August 2025, you can see that a slower P/E contraction is expected.

A slower P/E ratio contraction pace means one important thing - SOFI's share price is way ahead of its fundamentals. The company's valuation grew faster than its long-term EPS growth potential, meaning that there is a disconnect between share price movement and the business's intrinsic value. Another fascinating fact about SOFI's valuation is that if we zoom out and look at its earnings revisions since 2021, we will see that the company's EPS growth potential has actually shrunk.

The above chart is pure gold, in my opinion. In late December 2021, Wall Street analysts expected SOFI to generate $0.95 EPS in FY2026 and $1.69 in FY2030. Today, consensus forecasts a much lower EPS of $0.6 in the next fiscal year and just $1.26 in FY2030. If we compare these four numbers, we will find out that the FY2026 EPS forecast has declined by 36% and the FY2030 EPS forecast declined by around 25%. Meanwhile, SOFI's share price has doubled since late 2021 which is absolutely against any logic. I understand that the notable increase in the number of outstanding shares (0.8 billion in late 2021 versus 1.2 billion now) was larger than the EPS forecast declines, meaning that expectations grew in absolute numbers. However, why should it matter if we are speaking about price per share? Yes, the company's growth potential in absolute numbers expanded, but what is the value for a single retail investor if this growth potential is now shared by a far larger number of investors?

Moreover, yesterday's news that SOFI launched a large $1.5 billion public offering of common stock means another round of shareholder value dilution. The size of the offering represents around 4% of the company's current market cap, which is significant. The timing of such a move appears questionable, as it might look like the management is likely just capitalizing on skyrocketed valuation. In the release, it says that proceeds will be used "for general corporate purposes, including strengthening capital, improving capital management flexibility, and funding growth opportunities." Why would they do it if SoFi's balance sheet is already quite strong with $3.2 billion in cash and there are no rumors regarding potential acquisitions? Moreover, during the Q3 earnings call, the company's CFO said that "the balance sheet is strong with high-quality loans, excess capital, and solid liquidity". The Q3 earnings call took place in late October, and today is just early December, just slightly more than a month passed, and SOFI's "excess capital and solid liquidity" suddenly deteriorated? Additionally, phrases like "general corporate business" sound vague and unexplainable for a company with an asset-light business model and which is already bottom-line profitable. Meanwhile, insiders continue selling their SOFI shares, which is also a warning sign when paired with the equity raise.

Finally, let's not forget that SOFI is a financial services company. Its growth potential and financial well-being heavily depend on the macroenvironment, which does not look perfect at the moment. The U.S. economy is growing, but its shape appears to be far from perfect, as the growth was mostly driven by unprecedented investments in AI infrastructure. According to economist Mark Zandi, 22 states out of 50 are already in recession. This conclusion is based on the fact that these states demonstrate "slumping farm economies and slowing manufacturing industries". I think that "recession" is a very serious term, and I will not be using it. However, interest rates in the U.S. are still high, trade wars are still not over, and the unemployment rate continues ticking up. I know that higher interest rates can benefit SOFI's lending margins, but the effect is partially (or maybe entirely) offset by the suppressed loan demand due to high rates. This setup might not be scary enough to speak about the recession, but it is unlikely to trigger accelerated economic growth. In such a challenging environment, it seems overly optimistic to expect a financial services company like SOFI to deliver large positive surprises that might justify its valuation and aggressive dilution.

Reasons to invest in SOFI

Investors might be betting on SOFI's longer-term potential beyond FY2030. It is a risky bet because the longer the horizon, the greater the uncertainty. While I think that SOFI is overvalued as its EPS estimates are going nowhere, I also have to give credit to the management. They are quite aggressive in expanding the company's financial ecosystem, which might create an unexpected multiplicative effect, as more services offered can create cross-sell opportunities. For example, the company recently launched SoFi Crypto as part of its blockchain push. The digital assets industry is vast, which might create new growth opportunities for SOFI. I think that it might only be a remote growth catalyst for the company because we see that the digital assets market has declined notably in recent weeks, which might mean a new bearish phase for the crypto market.

I also have to acknowledge that the momentum is very strong, which always attracts more interest to the stock from investors. Depending on the broader market's sentiment, the robust share price momentum together with the expanding ecosystem of services might be enough to spur a rally further. If another FOMO wave triggers a new rally strong enough to break the $30 resistance level, a highly volatile stock like SOFI can easily soar to $40. It will make my bearish call look very bad. However, buying the stock because you believe in FOMO and not in fundamentals is speculation, not investing.

The verdict

SOFI's recent new $1.5 billion dilution round does not look sound given the company's robust balance sheet and rapidly improving bottom-line profitability. The company's shareholder value growth potential on a per share basis has gone nowhere since late 2021, yet the stock price soared by almost two times.
